title: Beyond Worst-Case Analysis in Online Learning
speaker: Tim Roughgarden
abstract:
One of the primary goals of the mathematical analysis of algorithms is to provide guidance about which algorithm is the “best” for solving a given computational problem. Worst-case analysis summarizes the performance profile of an algorithm by its worst performance on any input of a given size, implicitly advocating for the algorithm with the best-possible worst-case performance. Strong worst-case guarantees are the holy grail of algorithm design, providing an application-agnostic certification of an algorithm’s robustly good performance.
However, for many fundamental problems and performance measures, such guarantees are impossible and a more nuanced analysis approach is called for. Research in "beyond worst-case analysis" develops alternatives to worst-case analysis, with applications ranging from clustering to linear programming to neural network training. The first part of this talk will provide a general introduction to the area. The second part of this talk will describe an application to a fundamental problem in learning theory: regret-minimization in online learning. Worst-case online learnability has long been known to be characterized by the Littlestone dimension of the class of allowable hypotheses (which, for example, is infinite already for the class of one-dimensional threshold functions). Our main result here shows that this characterization is brittle in the sense that, with a "smoothed" adversary (with adversarially chosen inputs subjected to small random perturbations), online learnability is instead characterized by the VC dimension of the hypothesis class (which, for the same example, is 1). Time permitting, we will also discuss applications of our proof techniques to an online version of the Komlos problem in discrepancy theory. 
Includes joint work with Nika Haghtalab (UC Berkeley) and Abhishek Shetty (MIT), published in Journal of the ACM (2024).

proof_stages.label: Motivation for smoothed beyond-worst-case analysis
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts worst-case analysis with more nuanced beyond-worst-case models, introduces smoothed analysis as a semi-random interpolation, and identifies brittle hard instances plus a large average/worst-case gap as its natural use case.

proof_stages.label: Sequential prediction, regret, and VC-dimension benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The online binary prediction problem is formulated via regret against the best hypothesis in hindsight, and VC dimension is presented as exactly governing fast learning in the benign IID setting.

proof_stages.label: Failure of VC dimension under adaptive worst-case adversaries
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that adaptive adversaries make bounded VC dimension insufficient, with worst-case regret governed instead by Littlestone dimension, creating the endpoint gap that a smoothed theorem should bridge.

proof_stages.label: Sigma-smoothed adaptive adversary and main regret theorem
proof_stages.summary: The adversary is weakened only by requiring each adaptively chosen input distribution to have density at most 1/sigma times uniform, and the main theorem gives regret of order sqrt(VCdim(H) T log(1/sigma)) up to logarithmic factors, with tight sigma dependence.

proof_stages.label: Finite VC covers and multiplicative-weights reduction
proof_stages.summary: Bounded VC dimension supplies a finite epsilon-cover of the hypothesis class, allowing a standard Hedge/multiplicative-weights algorithm on the cover and reducing regret to a finite-class term plus an approximation-error term from disagreement regions.

proof_stages.label: VC concentration for disagreement-region errors
proof_stages.summary: For IID uniform data, Bernstein concentration and VC-size union bounds control how often the sequence falls in small disagreement regions, but the adaptivity and correlations of smoothed adversaries initially obstruct applying this directly.

proof_stages.label: Coupling smoothed adaptive samples to longer IID uniform samples
proof_stages.summary: A coupling dominates each sigma-smoothed adaptive draw by a block of IID uniform samples with high probability, enabling the IID concentration bounds to control approximation error and complete the tight smoothed regret bound.

examples.name: Simplex method and Klee-Minty cubes
examples.note: The linear-programming simplex method, together with Klee-Minty worst-case instances, motivates why brittle worst cases invite smoothed analysis.
